title: OOP Basics Quiz 🏆
description: Prove your structural mastery! Classes, Objects, Methods, and Constructors.

questions.question: What is the relationship between a Class and an Object?
options: A Class is real data, an Object is the template.
A Class is the blueprint template, an Object is the physical item built from it.
They are two separate programming languages.
questions.explanation: You write a Class once as a blueprint. You spawn multiple Objects from that blueprint.

questions.question: What do we call variables that are defined inside a Class to hold its data?
questions.explanation: Data points attached to a Class are called Properties or Fields.

questions.question: What do we call functions that are defined inside a Class?
questions.explanation: A Method is simply a function that belongs specifically to an object.

questions.question: To convert a Class blueprint into a real Object, you write the class name followed by parentheses: Player p1 = Player___;
questions.explanation: Calling the class name like a function `()` invokes the constructor and builds the object.

questions.question: How do you access a specific property of an object named `myCar`?
questions.explanation: The dot (.) operator is the standard way to dig into an object's properties or methods.

questions.question: If you create 3 different `Player()` objects, they all share the exact same `health` variable.
questions.explanation: False! Every single object gets its OWN unique, independent copy of the properties.

questions.question: What is a Constructor?
options: A function that deletes an object.
A special setup function that runs exactly once when an object is first created.
questions.explanation: The constructor acts as the initializing factory, setting up the object using parameters passed in.

questions.question: In Dart, a Constructor MUST exactly match the name of the Class it belongs to.
questions.explanation: Yes, `class Enemy` must have a constructor named `Enemy(...)`.

questions.question: Fill in the keyword that allows an object to refer to itself, preventing naming collisions: ___.name = name;
questions.explanation: The 'this' keyword points to 'this specific instance of the object'.

questions.question: What does this shortcut constructor do? `Car(this.color, this.speed);`
options: Throws an error because it has no body {}.
Automatically takes the arguments passed in and slots them directly into the object's properties with the same names.
questions.explanation: Dart's syntactic sugar automatically handles initialization if you use `this.` in the constructor parameters.

questions.question: A Class can contain multiple different Methods to perform various actions.
questions.explanation: A Class can have as many methods and properties as it needs to represent the object.

questions.question: Inside a class method, how do you modify one of the class's properties?
options: You must pass it as a parameter.
You can just type the property name directly, because the method already lives inside the object.
You cannot modify properties from inside a method.
questions.explanation: Methods inherently have access to all peer properties within the same class scope.

questions.question: In `class Hero { String name = 'Blank' }`, what's wrong with the code?
options: Nothing, this is perfectly valid syntax.
questions.explanation: Property declarations, like all statements in Dart, must end with a semicolon `;`.

questions.question: Why do we capitalize Class names (like String, List, Player)?
options: Because it is required by the Dart compiler.
It is a strict community convention to distinguish Classes/Objects from standard variables (like int or var).
questions.explanation: By convention, all Object-Oriented languages capitalize Class names to prevent developer confusion.

questions.question: If you have `Car c1 = Car();` how do you execute its `honk()` method?
questions.explanation: The dot operator accesses methods and executes them with parentheses.

questions.question: Objects can be stored inside a List! Examples: `List<Player> team = [p1, p2];`
questions.explanation: Absolutely! Objects are just custom data types. You can put them in Lists, Sets, and Maps.

questions.question: Can you call a Method of an object from *inside* another Method of that *same* object?
options: Yes, methods within the same class can freely call each other.
No, methods can only be called from main().
questions.explanation: Yes! `this.takeDamage()` can trigger `this.playDeathAnimation()` within the same object perfectly.

questions.question: If you specify parameters in a custom constructor, what happens to the default empty `()` constructor?
options: It vanishes; dart now forces you to provide the new arguments or the program won't compile.
questions.explanation: Once you define a custom constructor, Dart overrides the default empty one to enforce your new rules.

questions.question: The 'this' keyword can be used inside `main()`.
questions.explanation: No! 'this' specifically means 'this object'. Since `main()` is not an object, 'this' makes no sense there.

questions.question: What is the primary power of Object-Oriented Programming?
options: It bundles related Data and Behaviors into coherent real-world concepts.
questions.explanation: OOP perfectly models the real world by grouping relevant properties and methods into discrete encapsulated Objects.

questions.question: If `Dog d = Dog();` modifies a property `d.isHungry = false;`, does it affect `Dog d2 = Dog();`?
questions.explanation: Each Object instantiated from a Class occupies its own entirely unique space in memory.

questions.question: Methods inside a Class must always have a `void` return type.
questions.explanation: Methods are just functions. They can return `int`, `String`, `bool`, or even complex Objects.

questions.question:
Fill in the blank to build an object based on the `Enemy` class.
Enemy boss = __();
questions.explanation: You construct the object by calling the Class's name exactly as if it were a function.

questions.question: A Class is basically a complex, custom Data Type.
questions.explanation: Yes! Just as `String` holds characters, `Player` holds whatever data you designed it to hold.

questions.question: What happens when you mistakenly type `playerHealth.name = 'Link';` instead of `player.name = 'Link';`?
options: Compilation error: an integer type (playerHealth) does not have a property called 'name'.
questions.explanation: The dot operator only discovers Properties that were explicitly defined inside that specific Object's Class.
